title: Chapter 34: The Exposure
content: The champagne tastes like victory.

I'm standing in the center of the mezzanine, the showcase space transformed into something gilded and breathless — candles floating in shallow pools, white flowers spilling over every surface, the city winking through floor-to-ceiling glass forty stories below. Someone has pressed a flute into my hand for the third time, and I'm actually drinking it. I'm actually letting the bubbles hit my nose, letting the warmth bloom through my chest, letting the congratulations wash over me like something I deserve.

"Utterly transformative." That's Elena Voss — no relation to Maren, I checked months ago — the fashion director whose opinion could crater careers with a single raised eyebrow. She's gripping my forearm between both her hands, her rings cool against my skin. "The way you pulled those historical silhouettes into something so contemporary. The draping on the fourteenth look — I've never seen anything like it."
"Thank you." My voice is steady. My smile is real. "That one almost didn't make it to the runway."

"It should have opened the show." She squeezes once more and moves on, and I'm left holding my champagne and the strange, weightless feeling of having done the thing. The impossible thing. The collection that was supposed to be my undoing is instead the moment everything changed, and I am — for the first time in longer than I can calculate — allowing myself to stand inside it.
The hum is quiet. A low, contented vibration, like a cat purring somewhere deep in my sternum. Not a warning. Not alarm. Just the hum agreeing with the rest of me: *we did this. This is ours.*

More hands to shake. More names I'll forget tomorrow. A designer from Maison Margiela tells me I've "redefined the conversation," and I nod and smile and feel the champagne doing its work, softening the edges of a vigilance I've held for so long I forgot it was optional. The room is warm. The light is golden. My dress — my own design, black silk that moves like water — catches the candlelight and throws it back, and I feel beautiful and seen and *safe*.
I lift my flute for another sip and see him.

Dimitri Valk is standing at the bar. Not leaning — standing. Perfectly vertical, perfectly still, his posture the kind of controlled stillness that costs effort. He's not drinking. The bartender has polished the same glass three times rather than interrupt him. He's not watching the room. He's not networking, not performing the social choreography of an afterparty.
He's waiting.

Our eyes meet across forty feet of champagne and flowers and celebration, and the champagne on my tongue turns sour. Not vinegary — something worse. Something chemical and wrong, like the taste of a dream curdling at the edge. I swallow anyway. I don't look away. I won't give him that.
He holds up his phone.

The screen is bright enough to read from here — he wants me to see it. A document. Letterhead I recognize instantly because I've been on its payroll for three years: the Ashford Foundation logo, that austere serif, the weight of institutional authority rendered in ink and pixels. Below the letterhead: my name. ORLANDO. And under it, a list — or rather, the absence of one. Medical records. Or the white space where medical records should be.
He smiles.
It's not kind.
The hum shifts. Not the contented purr of moments ago — something sharper, a key turning in a lock that shouldn't exist. I set my champagne flute on the nearest surface. I don't trust my hands.
He's walking toward me.
And he's not alone.

Maren Voss materializes from the crowd like she was always part of it, stepping into stride beside Dimitri with the ease of someone who has rehearsed this entrance. The alliance is visible now — no longer subtext, no longer suspicion. They are a unit, marching toward me with aligned purpose, and the room full of industry heavyweights parts for them without knowing why. People sense these things. The predator's trajectory. The incoming weather.

Dimitri stops three feet away. Up close, his cologne hits me — oud and something aggressive underneath, camphor maybe, the sharp green of a plant that doesn't want to be touched. It's the scent of a man who leads with his presence and makes sure you breathe it.
"Magnificent show." His voice is warm. Genuinely admiring, which makes it worse. "Truly. The historical references were haunting."
A beat. His eyes hold mine.
"Almost too authentic. Like you were there."
The double meaning is a scalpel. Not a sledgehammer — he's too precise for that. A scalpel, slipping between ribs, finding the exact spot where the skin is thinnest.
I keep my face still. I have had centuries of practice. "Thank you."
"I have some questions about your medical records." He says it like he's asking about the weather, like it's a curiosity, a cocktail-conversation tidbit. "Or rather, the absence of them."
The hum ratchets tighter.

"Before 2019," he continues, "you don't seem to exist. No birth certificate. No school records. No immunizations." He tilts his head, studying me the way a scientist studies something on a slide. "It's as if you appeared out of thin air."

The champagne is definitely sour now. I can taste it at the back of my throat, acid and wrong, and I want another drink to wash it away but I will not move. I will not give him the satisfaction of watching me reach for something, anything, to steady myself.
"Records get lost," I say. "You know that better than anyone. You've written about archival gaps in three different columns."
"Archival gaps." He repeats it like he's tasting the phrase. "Charming euphemism."
Maren steps forward.

She's carrying a tablet — slim, dark, held at her side like a weapon at rest. She raises it now, and the screen glows blue-white in the candlelight, harsh and clinical against all this warmth and gold. The glow catches the planes of her face, makes her look like she's built from data and strategy and nothing soft.
"And then there are these."
She turns the tablet toward me.
The file is labeled in caps, in a font that screams urgency: ORLANDO — IMPOSSIBLE.

I've never seen this file. I've never known this file existed. But I know what it contains before the first image loads because my hum is *roaring* now — not a warning, not the careful tingle of danger approaching. A defense mechanism, something deeper than thought, older than strategy, an alarm system wired into whatever I am that screams *THREAT THREAT THREAT* until my vision tunnels and the edges of the room go gray and there's nothing in the world but this screen and what's on it.

The first photograph. Oil on canvas, eighteenth century. A woman in a garden, her waist cinched, her hair piled high, her hand resting on the stone railing of a bridge. Her face. My face. The same cheekbones, the same jaw, the same slight asymmetry at the left corner of the mouth that no painter would invent and no coincidence would replicate.
Maren swipes.

The second photograph. Black and white, grainy, wartime. A woman in a wool coat standing in a ruined street, the rubble of a city behind her, something unfathomable in her expression — grief? recognition? She is looking at the camera like she knows it's there. Like she's been photographed before and will be photographed again and knows exactly how long this moment will last.
My face. My face. My face.
"Same bone structure." Maren's voice is clinical, detached, a pathologist reading a chart. "Same face. For three hundred years."

The hum is so loud now I can barely hear her. It's inside my skull, behind my eyes, vibrating in the spaces between my thoughts. My hands are at my sides and I will not move them, I will not let them see me shake, but the hum is doing something to me that I can't name — pressing against the inside of my skin like something trying to get out, or keep something in, and my peripheral vision is swimming.

"Now," Maren says, and her tone shifts — not softer, but reasonable, the reasonable tone of someone offering you a door while locking every window. "We're reasonable people. If there's an explanation — a genetic condition, a family resemblance spanning generations — we'd love to hear it."
She lets the silence stretch.
"But if there isn't..."

She doesn't finish the sentence. She doesn't need to. The threat isn't in the words — it's in the white space where the words should be, the same white space where my medical records should be, the same white space where the first two hundred years of my life should be. My whole existence is white space now, and they've found the edges of it, and they're pressing.
"This conversation is over."
Rowan.

He's crossed the room — I don't know when, I don't know how, I was too lost in the screen and the hum and the faces that are my face — and now he's standing between me and the alliance, his back to me, his shoulders squared. He's in his charcoal suit, the one he wore for the showcase, and his hands are at his sides but his posture is a wall. He's put himself in front of me like I'm something worth shielding, and the tenderness of that instinct is almost worse than the threat because I know — I know — he can't stop what's already in motion.
"Rowan." Dimitri's smile widens. He was expecting this. He wanted this. "Always the protector."
"This conversation is over," Rowan repeats. Quieter now. The kind of quiet that makes people lean in.

"Is it?" Dimitri pulls a small, theatrical pause. He's enjoying this — the power, the timing, the way the room has narrowed to just the four of us while a hundred people celebrate twenty feet away, oblivious. "Because I have a column going live tomorrow morning. 'The Ashford Foundation's Impossible Designer.'" He lets the headline breathe. "It's going to ask some very uncomfortable questions about due diligence and corporate credibility."
Rowan doesn't flinch. "If you publish unsubstantiated claims about a Foundation designer, my legal team will respond within the hour."
"Unsubstantiated." Dimitri rolls the word around his mouth like a marble. "Who said anything about unsubstantiated?"

He holds up his phone again. This time he doesn't bother aiming it at me — he aims it at Rowan, letting him see the full extent of what they've assembled. The medical records — the absence of them, the void where a human life should be documented. The photographs. The centuries of photographic and painted evidence, the same face cycling through eras like a ghost caught in a loop. And something else — a letter. I catch the header: Venice. A date. A name I almost recognize, someone who almost knew me, someone who almost left a record.
"This is evidence, Rowan." Dimitri's voice is almost gentle. "Your own Foundation's records."

Rowan doesn't move. He doesn't deny it. He doesn't say *that's fabricated* or *that's out of context* because he can't — it's not fabricated, and there is no context that makes three hundred years of the same face explicable. The evidence is real. It came from his own institution, his own archives, the files he guards with varying degrees of awareness, and now it's in the hands of two people who know exactly what they have.

Maren steps around Rowan. Not aggressively — she doesn't need to be aggressive. She moves like someone who has already accounted for every obstacle, and Rowan is an obstacle, not a barrier. She meets my eyes directly, and there's something in her expression that might be sympathy or might be the performance of it.
"You have two choices, Orlando."
Her voice is calm. Reasonable. The trap closing with all the courtesy of a well-mannered executioner.
"You can tell the world yourself. Your story, in your words. On your terms." She pauses. "Or we tell it for you. And our version won't be kind."
The room is spinning.

Not from the champagne — I've barely had half a glass and my tolerance would require considerably more than that. It's the hum. The hum is building toward something I can't control, a pressure wave of soundless sound cresting behind my sternum, pushing outward against my ribs, against my skin, against the boundary between what I am and what the world is allowed to see. I've held it before. I've held it for centuries in worse circumstances than this. But the photographs — the faces — the evidence — the letter from Venice — something about seeing it all assembled, catalogued, *named*, has knocked loose a restraint I didn't know I was relying on, and the hum is not listening to me anymore.

Maren is still talking. Something about deadlines, editorial calendars, the twenty-four-hour news cycle. Dimitri is watching me with the faint smirk of a man who has already won. Rowan is between us, his hand reaching backward, finding my wrist, his fingers pressing against my pulse — he can feel it, the hum vibrating through my blood, he knows — and I'm trying, I'm *trying* to pull it back, to compress it, to make myself small enough to contain —
My skin shimmers.

Just once. Just a flash — a ripple of light that runs from my wrist where Rowan's fingers are pressed up my forearm, across my collarbone, along the line of my jaw. It's barely a second. It's the hum made visible, the inside showing through the outside, the thing I've spent my entire existence concealing breaking through the surface like a gasp.

It's electricity and it's *tactile* — I feel it as much as see it, a crackle across my nerve endings, a shiver that starts at the base of my skull and cascades down through every cell. My skin lights up with it. My silk dress catches it and throws it back. For one unspeakable moment, I am luminous.
Then it's gone.
Maren's eyes widen.

Not theatrical — not the practiced shock of a journalist scoring a headline. This is real. This is *organic*. Her pupils dilate and her mouth opens slightly and the tablet lowers an inch, forgotten, because whatever she suspected, whatever theory she constructed from photographs and records and absence, she was not prepared for visual confirmation. She thought she was chasing a fraud — identity theft, document forgery, some elaborate long-con that would make a story. She did not think she was chasing *this*.
Dimitri's smirk falters.

He saw it too. The shimmer registered in his eyes, in the way his chin pulled back a quarter inch, in the way his weight shifted from smug repose to something closer to — not fear, not yet, but the precursor to fear, the moment when the brain admits that the categories it brought to this encounter do not apply. He's not prepared for genuine impossibility. No one is. You can't prepare for the thing that doesn't fit in the world you've built.
The silence lasts two seconds. It feels like a century — I would know.

Maren recovers first. She always will. She's built for this — for the rapid absorption of the unprecedented, for retrofitting her strategies around new information. Her expression closes like a curtain, and when it opens again, it's calculated. She's recalibrating.
"We saw that," she says. Quietly. Not a threat — a confirmation.

Dimitri hasn't moved. His phone is still in his hand, the evidence still glowing, but the phone is no longer the most important thing in this room. I am. The shimmer — my body's testimony, involuntary and absolute — is the most important thing in this room, and now two people who want to destroy me have seen it, and *they know*.
They know they're not dealing with a fraud.
They're dealing with something they can't explain.
And I can't take it back.

Rowan's fingers tighten on my wrist. His pulse is fast too — I can feel it where his wrist presses against mine — and he's looking at Maren and Dimitri with an expression I've never seen on his face before: something that lives in the neighborhood of grief, but sharper, more immediate. The grief of having failed to prevent the unpreventable.

The champagne flute is still on whichever surface I abandoned it on. The candles are still floating. The party continues around us, oblivious, a hundred guests who have no idea that the world just shifted under their feet. That the rules they live by — birth, death, the linear march of a single human life — just developed a crack.

Maren's tablet glows between us, the photographs still on its screen. My face. Century after century. And now the shimmer — the *proof* — embedded in the memory of two people who will use it with surgical precision.
Dimitri finds his voice. It's quieter than before. Less theatrical. "Well," he says. "That changes things."
It does. It changes everything.
And I have never, in all my long and impossible life, been more afraid.
